data.title: Venture Global: Still Too Expensive To Pull The Trigger
data.description:
Venture Global (VG) is an LNG (liquefied natural gas) export company with ambitions to grow into the largest such company in the world. While the company is profitable, there are risks to this thesis that make holding the public equity of the parent company questionable even at $8 per share. I rate it a sell, but would consider buying if it completely washed out. With that as a preamble, let’s dive into the details.

Intro to LNG Exporting Companies

LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas) export companies use massive, industrial-scale refrigeration systems to cool natural gas. Because the gas must reach a temperature of approximately -160°C (-260°F) to turn into a liquid, the equipment used is far more complex than a standard refrigerator. In the industry, the specific unit where this cooling happens is called a Liquefaction Train. In liquid form, natural gas is 1/600th the volume of its gaseous form. Shrinking it to that scale allows the natural gas to be loaded and transported on LNG export ships to various regasification facilities around the world, which, through a reverse process, turn that liquid back into gas and inject it into the local pipeline network.

In short, an LNG export facility allows natural gas, which is abundant and cheap in North America, to be exported to Europe, Asia, South America, and anywhere else that needs it. As we shall see, building an LNG export facility is capital intensive and requires enormous injections of cash to build. In turn, the bondholders require contractual arrangements that typically run for 20 years. Anything less than 20-year agreements makes handing over $10+ billion a skittish affair.

VG's Strategy

Venture Global has proven that they can sign enough customers to long-term contracts to satisfy bondholders and build facilities at scale (on time and reasonably on budget), and this makes them a frontrunner to become the largest LNG export company in the world. Their goal is to build facilities in Louisiana that will eventually export 100 MTPA – million tons per annum. A million tons of LNG is around 48 to 49 billion cubic feet, and 100 MTPA would be the equivalent of exporting 13.3 Bcf/d (billion cubic feet per day), which easily doubles the export capacity of the US LNG.

They currently have a self-reported export capacity of around 38 MTPA (about 30% of US export capacity) with another 28 MTPA in the construction phase for a total of 66.2 MTPA (about 8.8 Bcf/d). Behind that, they have another expansion in the construction phase – CP2 (Calcasieu Pass 2) and two more in development—CP3 and an expansion of Plaquemines. For a single company to export this much LNG is an extraordinary engineering and financial feat, and one way to accomplish that is to promise a lot of things to a lot of people, as we shall see.

The goal with commissioning each new facility, is to require less externally generated capital and to build bigger and faster than before. As noted in the chart, their recently completed Plaquemine facility is over 2 times bigger than Calcasieu Pass. Once phase 1 and 2 of CP2 are complete, it will require approximately $29B in total capital and include up to 30 MTPA of export capacity.

In addition to the LNG facilities themselves, they also own three completed pipeline header systems to feed those facilities: TransCameron, a 42 inch, 24 mile header system that feeds Calcasieu Pass, Gator Express, a 27-mile pipeline, 48 inch header system that feeds Plaquemine up to 3.9 Bcf/d of natural gas and a recently completed CP Express, a 91.4 mile, 48 inch pipeline that will feed CP2. To help feed gas into CP Express, they also co-developed and own 50% of Blackfin Pipeline, which is still in the construction phase but will be ready by the time CP2 is ready to receive feed gas. Blackfin is jointly owned by Whitewater and is a 193-mile extension of the Matterhorn Express Pipeline, which was completed in 2024. Matterhorn is owned by Whitewater and other midstream companies. Blackfin also has the potential to receive feed gas from Eiger Express, a massive 3.7 Bcf/d new pipeline that will move into the construction phase early in 2026 and is scheduled for a mid-2028 in service date.

On the waterfront, Venture Global owns 9 LNG vessels and has chartered two more, which collectively have the capacity to export 15% of their current LNG production. Most LNG exported from their facilities is chartered by their customers.

A Massive Stack of Capital

The “capital stack” for Venture Global to pay for all of this infrastructure is complex, but understanding it is key to understanding the value of owning the publicly traded stock. Here’s the published “simplified” organizational structure. One has to chuckle at the word “simplified,” because this is anything but simple.

One of the purposes of the complex structure is to define legally who or what entity can lay claim to each project’s income, and in the event of a default, which entity can lay claim to the associated assets. From the perspective of the public common equity holder, we can further simplify the structure as follows:

Starting at the top and working our way down: As of Oct 31st, 2025, there are 479.5MM shares of the publicly traded Class A common stock and 1,968.6MM shares of Class B (plus another 192.9MM shares for employees). The Class B stock is owned by insiders and other early investors, and these shares have 10x the voting power of Class A stock. The 3 million VG-LNG perpetual preferred shares have a value of $2.9B with a 9% per annum dividend payout. As a reminder, preferred shares rank senior to common shares.

The 9 million VG-CP Funding Preferred shares were part of an early $1.3B equity package that Stonepeak provided in 2019 to fund the Calcasieu Pass facility. The terms of these preferred shares are expensive with annual 1% escalators (after commissioning, which occurred earlier in 2025). Distributions are “paid-in-kind.” Paid-in-kind means that distributions are paid in additional shares rather than cash dividends. Currently this pays 11% per annum, and there are clauses that require all the available cash flow from Calcasieu to be payable to the preferred shares (to redeem all the additional distributed shares since inception) before any remaining cash flow from Calcasieu Pass can be paid to the common equity. Then in 2027, all available cash flow from Calcasieu Pass must go towards redeeming the remaining shares. I assume these shares will eventually be purchased by Venture Global using cash from operations or perhaps by issuing either debt or new preferred equity at more favorable terms.

Rounding out the equity portion, Stonepeak owns a 23% common equity stake in the Calcasieu Pass facility, with Venture Global owning the remaining 77%. The book value of the 23% share is $583MM. Stonepeak used to own a larger share of common shares in Calcasieu, but Venture Global repurchased some shares in preceding years.

Finally, there is $32.6B of debt occupying almost every layer of the company, from construction loans to build CP2, to facility level debt for Calcasieu Pass and Plaquemines, to pipeline debt for Blackfin, to $11B Senior Secured Notes of Venture Global LNG. Construction debt is running 8.8% in aggregate, and debt for finished projects is running 7.5%. The aggregated interest on all $32.6B is 8%, with interest for the construction loans being capitalized. Whew! That’s a lot of capital! Total enterprise value for Venture Global sits at $57B, with the common shares of Venture Global LNG making up 37% of that capital structure (as of Jan 13, 2026). Venture Global LNG seeks to own all or substantially all the equity ownership in its current five LNG projects and any future projects, which will simplify the structure.

How LNG Exporting Companies Get Paid

The next key piece for us to understand is how an LNG facility typically gets paid. A typical simplified sale might look something like this with costs listed per MMBtu of LNG sold:

Henry Hub is a natural gas pipeline header system located in Erath, Louisiana, and is the primary pricing benchmark for the North American natural gas market. For LNG contractual arrangements (whether short, medium, or long-term), feed gas is typically indexed to Henry Hub and delivered to the facility at “cost-plus.” The 15% markup covers the fuel use—both the fuel used to transport it to the facility and the fuel consumed in the liquefaction process. The Liquefaction Fee covers the facility’s operating and capital costs plus profit. Typically, the 20-year contractual costs for liquefaction fees range from $1.75 to $3.50 per MMBtu, depending on the contract, but they can also be sold on the spot market for higher amounts.

The Transportation and regasification costs cover the boat, fuel, tolls, docking fees, and the fees associated with regasification on the other side of the pond. In the example above, $4.86 is a very lucrative liquefaction fee, and more than covers the actual cost to liquefy natural gas and pay for the capital costs. In long-term contractual arrangements, the customer pays a much smaller liquefaction fee on a take-or-pay basis, and when the LNG is sold, they pocket the difference between the spot price for natural gas overseas and the contractual liquefaction fee. In spot market sales, the LNG exporting facility pockets the full amount—in our example, they earn the full $4.86 per MMBtu.

TTF is the Henry Hub of Europe—TTF stands for Title Transfer Facility. It is a virtual trading point for natural gas in the Netherlands and serves as the primary pricing benchmark for all of Europe. The key point to remember is that TTF (and the Asian version—JKM, Platts Japan Korea Marker) trades in line with Brent crude oil on a BTU basis, and Henry Hub trades at a substantial discount to Brent crude oil. The arbitrage between those two values is what makes LNG trading so profitable.

I suppose it’s possible that an LNG exporting facility could buy the gas for less than Henry Hub and therefore make an additional profit on the front end, but for Venture Global this turns out not to be case. At least not yet:

In fact, for the first 9 months of 2025, I calculate that their feed gas costs exceeded their cost structure by $0.23 per MMBtu, even with the 15% markup, so there is pricing risk embedded in the model. Blackfin pipeline, purchase agreements with natural gas producers in the US and becoming contractual shippers on pipelines that draw natural gas from major shale basins—these are all strategies to reduce that risk.

Since the feed gas costs are indexed to Henry Hub and the transportation and regasification fees are relatively fixed, the liquefaction fee is where the money is made, so the next key piece to understand is how liquefaction fees are determined. Venture Global’s strategy has been to price long-term contracts low to entice the rapid sign-up of customers, and they have been very successful in this endeavor.

For Calcasieu Pass, the average liquefaction fee earned in the third quarter of 2025 was $1.76/MMbtu, which is on the low side. The fourth quarter of 2025, saw this figure increase to $2.01 (due in part to a larger volume of spot cargoes sold), which is an improvement. These long-term contracts do have inflation escalators, but even so, these fixed fees seem brutally low, given the risk the facility is assuming and all the externally funded equity it raised, and the terms of its preferred equity as discussed above. If you then tack on the cost of pending arbitrations (more on this below), then this facility's cash flow could become even more burdened over time.

Calcasieu Pass is past their COD (commercial operation date), where LNG cargoes are now sold primarily on 20-year contracts with low fixed liquefaction fees. Their strategy is to sell 20-year contracts for all the nameplate capacity at these low fixed liquefaction rates and then sell the expected excess capacity at higher rates. (I believe that Plaquemine’s long-term contracts are priced closer to $2.30/MMBtu, and CP2 rates are even higher). Remember, long-term contracts satisfy the bondholders and VG’s speed in setting up facilities and acquiring funding can potentially crowd out a very competitive field of new LNG exporting facilities.

Calcasieu has 10 MTPA of nameplate capacity and 2.4 MTPA of potential excess capacity (although they only shipped about 0.2 MTPA of that excess capacity in the fourth quarter). Plaquemines has 20 MTPA of nameplate capacity and 7.2 MTPA of excess capacity (shipping around 1.5 MTPA of that excess capacity in the fourth quarter). As mentioned, Calcasieu is past its COD; Plaquemines is still commissioning and will reach the COD by the end of 2026 for phase 1 and early 2027 for phase 2. The commissioning phase allows Venture Global to sell their cargoes on the spot market and capture all the arbitrage. In the third quarter of 2025, the average fixed liquefaction fee for Plaquemine was a juicy $6.79/MMBtu, dropping to $6.02/MMBtu in the 4th quarter. Healthy profits from previous projects pay for the equity in new projects and allow them to start construction earlier. Consequently, this strategy of selling spot cargoes at rich premiums during the commissioning phase of each facility reduces the risk for investors across the board.

Putting a Price Tag on Venture Global

In terms of income and value of the common stock, we need to do some analysis, and this starts with the income statement. Net income attributable to common stockholders was $429MM in Q3 or $0.18 per undiluted share or $0.16 per diluted share. This equates to a price to earnings ratio of 12 or if you prefer to deal in percentages, it is yielding about 8%. Given that the S&P 500 is trading at a lofty 29 P/E for Q3 (as reported earnings) or 3.4%, you can comfortably place your buy order now, knowing the earnings will be substantially discounted to that. This is a reasonable placeholder, and it would make the stock a relative buy if this income had any legs. As a reminder, net income accounts for the cost of capital consumed (depreciation) and the future cost of cash income taxes, so this is a reasonable perspective. Using the diluted share count also allows for all the common stock that has to date been promised to employees.

For another view into earnings, we can look at the run rate EBITDA for Q4 2025 and note that they will earn approximately $1.94B in EBITDA (based on updated guidance that VG has provided) - annualized that’s $7.7B per year. That’s an enterprise value to EBITDA of 7.4 with anything less than 10 rated a buy. That’s the surface level analysis and maybe what is propping up the stock.

Eroding Profits

However, there are three problems with relying on these figures. The first is that these earnings are being heavily supported by the spot cargoes of Plaquemines and as stated above, these spot cargoes will become contracted at a much lower rate when Plaquemines reaches COD. The second problem concerns their spot cargoes. The prices for TTF and JKM have been falling throughout 2025 because their prices are tied to Brent and the international price of oil, and Brent has been falling because OPEC flooded the market with 2.2 million barrels per day of crude oil production in the span of 6 months. This is putting the world of crude oil in an oversupplied condition that will persist into 2026 and potentially beyond, and this oversupplied crude market has dragged down the global natural gas price with it.

The third problem is that Plaquemines and the newly commissioned Golden Pass facility (see Exxon Mobil—Better Returns Are Ahead) and other facilities are temporarily flooding the global market with natural gas. It is possible that some spot cargoes go unsold, or get sold at a discount, or further pressure the price of TTF and JKM. It is not inconceivable that the international price of natural gas temporarily decouples from the price of crude oil.

Let’s start with the forward curves for Henry Hub and TTF (as of Jan 13th) and use the future spread between those two figures to estimate the downdraft to earnings in 2026. The current 12-month Henry Hub strip is trading at $3.45/MMBtu, and TTF is trading at $9.60/MMBtu. If we add in the 15% feed gas cost and subtract the $1.21 in transportation and regassification fees, we are left with a $4.42 spot price for liquefaction based on the 12-month strip. The Q4 fixed liquefaction fee for Plaquemines was $6.02/MMBtu. The delta between those two numbers is $1.60/MMBtu on 26 MTPA or $2.2B in lost EBITDA for the full-year relative to our $7.7B annualized Q4 EBITDA figure. That puts the EBITDA estimate for 2026 based on the forward strip at $5.6B compared to a street estimate of $5.9B (with 16 analysts reporting as of Jan 13). CP2 is scheduled to begin shipping cargoes in Q3 of 2027 so that won’t affect the 2026 numbers.

Looking out to 2027, we can also note that once the COD dates are reached for Plaquemines phase 1 and 2, another $1.2B in EBITDA will be eroded as contracts move to the 20-year contractual rate for the nameplate capacity. The 2027 strip pricing for Henry Hub and TTF, puts the liquefaction fee at $3.29 or another $1.13/MMBtu less than the 2026 spread, which will slice off another $0.4B in EBITDA for the excess cargoes sold. That puts us at about $4.1B in EBITDA for Plaquemines and Calcasieu Pass and the other assets in service for 2027 plus some commissioning cargoes from CP2 if it is completed on time. Perhaps they can squeeze out a bit more from Plaquemines and Calcasieu.

The downdraft in earnings adds a lot of uncertainty to the stock price, especially as estimates are revised lower. Having said that, forward curves are not 100% predictors of future prices. TTF could rise over time (especially with geopolitical uncertainty), and Henry Hub could fall, widening the spread and alleviating some of the heartburn with these numbers.

If we look at the enterprise value side of the equation, we can note that $8.2B has been invested in CP2 and if we subtract that from $57B, we arrive at $48.5B. But then we come to the 5 pending arbitrations against Venture Global, which can add $4.8B to $5.5B in debt to pay for them. It is impossible to accurately predict the outcome (or the timing for payouts), but let’s add a $3B buffer and call the enterprise value for their current assets $51.5B excluding Blackfin and CP2. Our EV/EBITDA ratio for currently producing assets then becomes $51.5B divided by $4.1B or 12.6 (for producing assets), hardly a bargain. You could argue that more EBITDA is coming with the commissioning of CP2, but on the flip side, so is a lot more debt.

Conclusion

To sum up, we have a common stock in a company selling for about $8 per share (as of Jan 15th) with almost zero voting power and a tiny 1% dividend. It debuted at $25, but the price isn’t quite low enough to be considered a bargain. Their strategy to quickly bring inexpensive natural gas to the global market at scale for low prices is the very strategy that is undermining their margins over time. On the plus side, they are retaining a decent amount of operating income, which can help fund CP2. Perhaps over 3 to 5 years, they can slowly chip away at some of their debt and preferred equity and right the LNG ship.

data.title: Sarepta Therapeutics: Is Elevidys A Bust?
data.description:
It has been roughly seven months since my previous Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT) article titled “Sarepta Therapeutics: Rebuilding the Thesis After Hitting Rock Bottom.” At that time, SRPT was in collapse after ELEVIDYS had become a regulatory nightmare as reports of Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD) patients dying following treatment. ELEVIDYS went from being viewed as a once-in-a-generation franchise treatment to potentially a safety nightmare that looked like it was destined to be pulled from the market by regulators. Although I did have my concerns, I stressed that it looked like it was only going to be non-ambulatory patients that were going to be pulled from treatment, which would hurt their peak sales estimates, but the market was dismissing the long-term value of Sarepta’s Duchenne franchise. As a result, I believed SRPT was trading at a discount and decided to add to the position in the $15 handle in early August.

Seven months later, we now see that ELEVIDYS was not withdrawn but has black-box warnings, and Sarepta will likely have to deal with payer scrutiny, as well as lingering safety concerns. However, the main concern is that we are seeing a quarter-over-quarter decrease in ELEVIDYS sales. Indeed, the company just reported that their preliminary Q4 and 2025 numbers showed nearly $900M of ELEVIDYS revenue in 2025, pulling in roughly $1.86B in total product sales. Plus, they ended 2025 with almost $1B in cash. However, the market is obviously apprehensive about the fading ELEVIDYS sales and the Q4 “miss.”

It looks as if the same tension that has dictated SRPT for the past several years is still in control of the price action. The market has made a game out of constantly repricing SRPT based on headlines, which has provided plenty of trading opportunities for investors to manage their position around earnings reports, safety headlines, and commercial updates. On the downside, it makes SRPT a laborious investment as investors struggle to maintain long-term revenue models and remain vigilant for company updates. Well, the preliminary Q4/2025 numbers report could be another opportunity as the share price goes into another tailspin as the market attempts to analyze the “miss” and how ELEVIDYS will perform in the coming quarters.

I intend to provide some background on how Sarepta has become a franchise stock and how the fate of SRPT is tied to ELEVIDYS. Then, I will take a look at the preliminary numbers and will discuss how the “miss” on earnings might have investors wondering if ELEVIDYS is a bust. In addition, I will point out some bullish points from Sarepta’s presentation at the JPMorgan (JPM) Healthcare Conference. Plus, I will revisit my leading downside risk and how I plan to manage my SRPT position as we head into 2026.

The Drug Stock

I would claim that Sarepta’s stock over the past several years has been a result of investor confidence in the resilience of the company’s revenue base and the company’s R&D prowess in rare diseases, mainly DMD. The company’s earnings have evolved with their journey to becoming a DMD franchise company to what is now a company dependent on ELEVIDYS for explosive growth.

Indeed, the ticker was trading at a premium with only a fragmented exon-skipping DMD market, which was essentially overlaid by the dawdling, incremental growth of their PMO drugs. That dynamic created a rough environment for SRPT that would rally on positive clinical updates, but the gains would often fade as the Street questioned how large the business was really growing from the PMO therapies. ELEVIDYS changed that with early commercial uptake smashing through the company’s previous projected ceiling. ELEVIDYS was expected to be an option for most DMD patients, which would model a multibillion-dollar opportunity. That opportunity made SRPT transition from a DMD franchise stock to essentially an ELEVIDYS stock.

The volatility since then has simply reflected the market’s attempt to price that future correctly. So, when data, guidance, or commercial updates are positive, the stock moves substantially higher, but when safety concerns, manufacturing questions, or reimbursement issues pop up, or when sales start to slip, the stock drops. However, I must reiterate, the swings are not about whether Sarepta survives; they are more about the market’s prediction of ELEVIDYS.

Concerning Trends

Digging into Sarepta’s reported preliminary numbers, their full-year product revenue was $1.86B, with ELEVIDYS contributing $898.7M in its first full commercial year, while the PMOs combined for $965.6M. So, almost a 50/50 split in revenue for ELEVIDYS and the PMOs. However, when we look at the Q4 numbers, the product revenue was $369.6M, with ELEVIDYS contributing $110.4M, which means the PMOs collected $259.2M. So, the company’s revenue is now 30% ELEVIDYS.

Some have proposed that the SRPT sell-off was because analyst expectations had ELEVIDYS bringing in $130M to $150M in revenue for Q4, but they only recorded $110M, and investors were concerned about the “miss” on expectations. However, Sarepta publicized that six infusions were pushed from late December into early January due to the flu season. ELEVIDYS is $3M per infusion, so that is about $18M of deferred revenue going from Q4 of 2025 to Q1 of 2026. I’m not saying that a miss on the Street’s expectations was not a factor in the sell-off, but I think the commercial trends we are seeing from ELEVIDYS are probably a bigger factor.

Below, we have ELEVIDYS' quarterly numbers for 2025.

Q1: ~$375M

Q2: ~$282M

Q3: ~$131.5M

Q4: $110.4M

Clearly, we have to concede that the decrease in revenue is solely due to a drop in demand. We also have to consider that the Q1 to Q2 drop has to do with some normalization after a very strong Q1, which benefited from rapid early uptake. The Q2 to Q3 drop is likely due to the pause on non-ambulatory treatment and ongoing safety concerns. And the drop from Q3 to Q4 was attributed to flu season and safety-related rescheduling, rather than just pure erosion in ELEVIDYS demand.

Regardless of the reasons why, the market is not going to respond positively to a highly touted drug that has seen an astounding first quarter of launch and has only seen those numbers drop off in less than a year.

Is ELEVIDYS A Bust?

Despite the fading sales numbers, I think it is very hard to argue that ELEVIDYS is a bust when it brought in nearly $900M in first-year revenue, which is exceptional for a gene therapy. Yes, we have to revise our expectations for the near term, but Sarepta’s management reiterated a $500M annual revenue floor for ELEVIDYS, and they expect to exceed it. Although the preliminary earnings numbers are concerning, I don’t think it is absurd to expect normalization in the ambulatory population to improve enough to reach or exceed that $500M level.

Yes, we have to accept that the limitation to the ambulatory population has certainly limited peak sales for the time being, but a steady reversal to quarter-over-quarter growth could revitalize the bullish outlook for ELEVIDYS and SRPT.

I think Sarepta could rally investors if ELEVIDYS could hit these quarterly marks.

Q1: $130–150M

Q2: $160–180M

Q3: $180–200M

Q4: $200–230M

I know that I’m proposing a scenario that is well above the company’s estimated floor for ELEVIDY, but I do believe the company is going to easily surpass $500M and show clear upward momentum to get the market to firmly back SRPT. I must highlight that the company claims that around 80% of the ambulatory DMD remains untreated, so the company could still report strong growth with their current label.

Thankfully, the company has not given up on the non-ambulatory population despite the FDA’s revised indication. Sarepta is working on improved safety protocols to treat non-ambulatory patients again. The company has received the green light from the FDA to initiate Cohort 8 of the ENDEAVOR study, which is trying a boosted immunosuppressive treatment for non-ambulatory DMD patients for ELEVIDYS. Approximately 25 participants will receive sirolimus before and after gene therapy infusion to mitigate the risk of acute liver injury. The data would be expected in the second half of this year, but we could see some early safety data before then. Obviously, if the data is positive, we could see the FDA support resuming commercial dosing of ELEVIDYS in the non-ambulatory population towards the end of 2026 or 2027. Now, I must stress that estimates for the percentage of the U.S. DMD population that is ambulatory vs. non-ambulatory are inconsistent, with ranges going from around 1/3 to 1/2. So, getting the non-ambulatory patient population back could roughly double the addressable market in a year.

So, I would say ELEVIDYS is far from being a bust, with a strong first year, a possible resurgence in year two, and an acceleration in year three, which could help put the therapy back on track to a multi-billion dollar peak sales.

Bullish Highlights Beyond ELEVIDYS

While ELEVIDYS is understandably going to determine how investors treat SRPT, I must stress that Sarepta’s fundamentals remain strong. The company’s PMO franchise is still a remarkably underappreciated foundation that is still growing in value, but also in stability. Keep in mind, EXONDYS, VYONDYS, and AMONDYS together brought in nearly $1B in revenue in 2025. So, ELEVIDYS might not experience a huge inflection this year, but investors should feel reassured that the company’s PMOs have what it takes to maintain cash flow until the company unlocks the non-ambulatory population again.

I also need to highlight Sarepta’s financial positioning, which is roughly $954M in cash and investments and no near-term debt. So, I believe the company has the balance sheet and sustained cash flow to fund their siRNA pipeline.

My point is that Sarepta is at the stage where investors should see them as more than a single drug or therapy story. Admittedly, I don’t think the market will see it that way and will take SRPT where the ELEVIDYS outlook is pointing. However, fundamentally, Sarepta is now a self-funding rare-disease platform with a next-gen pipeline and has the cash position to exercise some strategic optionality… with or without ELEVIDYS hitting its estimated peak sales.

Risk Remains

Despite all my points above… I have to reiterate that SRPT is handcuffed to ELEVIDY for the foreseeable future, which will likely attract elevated volatility. Investors must accept that there is a possibility that sales numbers for ELEVIDYS could get worse before they get better. It is possible that the safety concerns will continue to linger, and patients or providers don’t want to risk the therapy. It is also possible that the non-ambulatory study fails, and that population will never be put back on the label. Even if ELEVIDYS gets back to full strength, we have to accept that the company is treating an incredibly fragile patient population, so adverse effects and deaths might be a permanent risk to any of the company’s therapies. Plus, you also have to consider the manufacturing of these gene therapies and the risk that payers become hesitant to continue supporting these therapies with multi-million dollar price tags.

However, in the face of these risks, I’m keeping my SRPT conviction rating at 3 out of 5, and the ticker will remain in the Compounding Healthcare “Bio Boom” portfolio.

My Game Plan

My SRPT approach will largely remain the same but will still demand a high degree of focus, discipline, and flexibility as we move deeper into 2026. I was successful in adding to the position in August, around $15 per share, which made me rethink placing small, incremental buy orders around the $25 level.

Now, I’m going to wait and see if the market wants to bring SRPT back down to $18 per share at some point in January, then $19 in February, and then $20 in March. If so, I will make a small addition for the first time in 2026. On the other hand, if the market starts to push SRPT higher, I will wait for the share price to climb above the anchored-VWAP from the June 2024-high before clicking the buy button.

In the end, the overall strategy remains unchanged: to actively trade SRPT to generate profits while collecting a long-term “house money” position that is funded by the market.

data.title: ADX: 8% Yield On Large Caps
data.description:
Looking for high-yield income from diversified large-cap stocks?

The Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX) is one of the oldest closed-end funds, "CEF's," in the US, having begun in 1929. It has been paying dividends since 1929.

Fund Profile:

"Adams Diversified Equity Fund, a closed-end fund, seeks to deliver superior returns over time by investing in a broadly diversified equity portfolio. The fund invests in a blend of high-quality, large-cap companies. The fund seeks to generate returns that exceed its benchmark as well as consistently distribute dividend income and capital gains to shareholders." (ADX site)

ADX is in the top 3 largest market cap equity CEFs, with $3.04B in Net Assets. It holds 94 positions, with an average daily volume of 302K, and no leverage.

Its annual expense ratio is just 0.50%, with no leverage.

Holdings:

Tech is by far the largest sector exposure, at 34.5%, followed distantly by Financials, 13%; Consumer Discretionary, at 10.7%; Communications Services, at 10.1%; Healthcare, at 9%; and Industrials, at 7.8%; and Consumer Staples, at 5%. The remaining sector exposures run from 1.6% to 3.2%:

As of 9/30/25, ADX's top 10 holdings included all the Magnificent 7 stocks, in addition to Broadcom, Chase, and Adams Natural Resources Fund, which gave ADX exposure to energy stocks. The top 10 positions accounted for ~43% of ADX's portfolio.

Dividends:

At its 1/14/26 closing price of $23.10, ADX yielded 7.97% - it pays a quarterly $0.46 distribution.

data.title: Kinder Morgan: The Setup Looks Easy, But It Isn't (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Kinder Morgan's Earnings Approach

Kinder Morgan (KMI) offers us exposure to key megatrends. I have been discussing natural gas and LNG over and over. Since it is a midstream operator, it is linked with two trends: domestic demand for natural gas is rising as a consequence of data centers and the manufacturing reshoring; LNG exports from the Gulf Coast to Europe and Asia. Its business model is strong because around 90% of its revenue is not directly linked to commodity prices, either coming from take-or-pay contracts or fees on volumes. As a result, the stock falls into the tollbooth category thanks to its steady cash flows. Kinder Morgan is one of the largest midstream operators in the U.S., since it moves around 40% of domestic gas. It usually reports EBITDA margins above 40%, which the company has wisely used to reduce its leverage to around 3.8x, which is acceptable for midstream. Two quarters ago, I initiated my KMI coverage with a neutral rating since I thought the company was already priced correctly when we looked at its multiples. It seems the market agreed, as the stock has been trading sideways since then, moving between $26 and $29.

As Kinder Morgan's Q4 2025 earnings approach, we have an interesting opportunity to update our research and see what we could be expecting from the company and whether we are before an opportunity to buy the stock at a reasonable price or not.

Kinder Morgan's Earnings Preview

Kinder Morgan's Q3 was a strong report, with EBITDA up 6% and adj. EPS up 16% YoY. The company confirmed that the natural gas business was outperforming the other businesses, accounting for over two-thirds of the overall budget.

By now, we know that Q4 is also expected to be strong. The consensus expects revenue growth of 8.6% and EPS growth above 14%. For midstream, these are fast-growing rates. So, unless an unexpected print is released, I don't think this will move the needle. As long as gas transport volumes grow above 4% and the leverage ratio stays below 4x, I don't think we will see a big reaction in either direction.

What matters about this report is the 2026 outlook. To this regard, Kinder Morgan issued a note in December where it said that it expects its FY26 EBITDA to grow 4% more than what it had forecasted at the end of Q3. In other words, a little over a month after it released its Q3 earnings, it had to increase its guidance by 4 full percentage points. This is a big change, and it confirms that Q4 should be strong. In addition to the quarterly changes, we also know that KMI has committed more than $9B to expansion projects (two years ago, it was at $3B), which shows how real the gas demand surge is.

All estimates believe that by 2030, natural gas demand will reach 28 Bcf/day, a 21.4% increase versus 2024. This is sustained growth for a commodity such as gas. This leads us to capex and interest rates. Most of us believe that this year we will see interest rates go down, either moderately or steeply. This will be an advantage for a company such as KMI because it will lower the cost of its debt and its interest expense. Just to give you an idea of how much its leverage can weigh on it, a 0.1x reduction equals around $850M. This is why KMI has indeed been growing its dividend but has been opportunistic with its buyback policy, choosing to reduce its leverage before committing to big share repurchase programs.

Kinder Morgan's Valuation

Overall, I like the company's business; I like its exposure to natural gas and LNG; I like its domestic footprint and the assets it operates. I think for several years it could truly perform well. This positions the company as an interesting stock to own. Let's then look at the valuation multiples to see whether it is a buy or not ahead of the earnings release after the close on Wednesday, January 21.

Right now, KMI trades at a fwd P/E of 21.4, a fwd EV/EBITDA of 11.4, and a fwd P/FCF of 10.4. The dividend yield is 4.25%, and the FCF yield is 4.5%. In a certain way, these multiples make the stock appear as a bond-proxy. The multiples are not too expensive, but they are not cheap either. Its cash flows behave similarly to bonds, but there are some differences. For example, when rates move down, this inflates KMI's stock price, whereas bonds usually lose value. Secondly, KMI has an underlying business that can grow and can report surprises. More or less, these multiples tell us that investors are confident about KMI's current cash flows and their moderate growth, but don't expect a rapid acceleration. This makes sense, since the increase in the project backlog will inevitably impact the company's FCF generation. In this scenario, small beats don't matter, while misses can trigger a rerating if they are a consequence of altered costs or leverage. It is a setup where good news appears but stops being enough to drive the stock up and out of the range that has caught it in recent months.

Right now, I think the chance for KMI to hold its value and keep paying dividends is high because the setup I described points in that direction. However, it is not the ideal situation for a new investor to jump aboard. It also doesn't look like a momentum trade, as this earnings report seems to me more a confirmation quarter, rather than a make-or-break moment. After all, right now Treasuries offer the same yield and (probably) a similar stability. KMI doesn't seem to be poised to exit from its current trading range unless a new catalyst appears (i.e., big interest rate cuts). As much as I like the company, my rating on the stock is still neutral.

data.title: J.M. Smucker: Buy This Dividend Aristocrat While It's On Sale
data.description:
Successful investing doesn’t have to be a popularity contest. Instead of chasing growth, I’m more content with pursuing value stocks that are unpopular with the market today so that I can get bigger gains in the future. In the words of football great Jerry Rice: “Today I will do what others won’t, so tomorrow I can do what others can’t.” What’s even better is if the value stock pays you to wait with a decent dividend income stream.

This brings me to J.M. Smucker (SJM), which I last covered in October, highlighting its growing key brands, innovation, and expanding market reach. SJM remains in value range at the current price of $104.78. As shown below, it sits in the bottom half of its 52-week range with a forward P/E of 11.6 and a 4.2% dividend yield.

In this article, I revisit SJM, including recent business results, and discuss why it’s a value and income "Buy" at present for potentially strong total returns, so let’s dig in!

Why SJM?

J.M. Smucker is a consumer staples giant that serves markets across North America. It has a leading product portfolio that covers coffee, peanut butter, fruit spreads, frozen handhelds, sweet baked goods, and dog and cat food. Its notable brands include Folgers, Dunkin, Café Bustelo, Jif, Uncrustables, Smucker’s, Hostess, Milk-Bone, and Meow Mix.

It’s fairly well known by now that consumer staples names like General Mills (GIS), Campbell’s Company (CPB), and Hormel (HRL) have faced margin pressures due to higher costs of goods and price sensitivity among consumers. SJM is no different, with operating margin falling from the +18% level to 16.5% over the trailing 12 months. Nonetheless, SJM’s operating margin still sits above the 12% level from 2023, and revenue has remained stable after growing materially in 2024, as shown below.

SJM produced decent top-line growth during fiscal Q2 2026 (ended Oct. 31, 2025) with net sales rising by 3% YoY to $2.3 billion. Excluding divestitures and foreign currency exchange, comparable net sales grew by a more appealing 5% over the prior year period. Sales growth was driven by an 11 percentage point increase in prices, particularly for strong growing brands like Café Bustelo. This more than offset a 6 percentage point decrease in volume in products, as shown below, driven by lower coffee, peanut butter, and dog snacks volume.

At the same time, higher commodity costs and an unfavorable product mix (more sales of lower-margin products) resulted in a gross profit decline of 2 percent. Despite challenges in volume during fiscal Q2, SJM’s market share has been improving, particularly in Sweet Baked Snacks and the Hostess brand (acquired in late 2023). SJM is also seeing sequential improvement in the convenience store channel. This is driven by portfolio simplification, in which 25% of SKUs were eliminated in favor of focusing on core brands like SJM’s market-leading Donuts, Donettes, and Cupcakes.

Looking ahead, SJM is positioning itself to stabilize demand and grow margins through higher brand investment this year. At the same time, management continues to streamline operations, including the planned closure of the Indianapolis facility by fiscal Q4, which is expected to save $30 million in annual run rate costs. SJM is also unlocking value through brand synergies by introducing Peanut Butter Bites to Milk-Bone brand through a collaboration with Jif.

Importantly, the fast-growing Uncrustables brand continues to be a growth driver for the company. This is reflected by 7% sales growth in the Away From Home category (including Uncrustables). SJM intends to grow Uncrustables to be a $1 billion brand with new recent offerings, including peanut butter and chocolate called PB Choco Craze, and two new higher protein items in the product pipeline. Notably, the U.S. protein market is expected to grow at a robust 8.8% CAGR through 2034 to $6.1 billion, as shown below.

SJM is currently moderately leveraged after its recent acquisitions, including Hostess Brands. Management expects the net debt-to-leverage ratio to be around 4x for the balance of fiscal 2026. With debt paydown, they expect leverage to step down toward the safer 3x level in fiscal 2027. This appears to be a credible plan to ratings agency S&P as SJM maintains a BBB credit rating.

Importantly for income investors, SJM currently yields 4.2%, and the dividend is well-covered by a 49% payout ratio, leaving plenty of room for debt paydown and brand portfolio investments. Notably, SJM is a Dividend Aristocrat with 28 consecutive years of raises under its belt and a 5-year dividend CAGR of 4.1%. As shown below, SJM’s dividend yield currently sits at the high end of its 10-year range.

I see value in SJM at the current price of $104.78 with a forward P/E of 11.6, which, as shown below, sits far below its historical P/E of 15.9. SJM’s valuation also sits well under the consumer staples sector median P/E, which also happens to be 15.9.

At the current valuation, I believe the market is too bearish around SJM’s recent headwinds and margin pressures. The profitability issue may stabilize over the course of this year through the aforementioned portfolio and process simplification. At the same time, growth brands like Café Bustelo and Uncrustables should continue to drive growth for SJM. Analysts estimate 7% to 9% annual EPS growth over the next 2 fiscal years. This, combined with the 4.2% dividend yield and potential for a reversion to the mean valuation, could result in total annual returns in the mid-teens.

Risks to SJM include its higher leverage ratio at present while it also plans to invest in R&D and marketing. This exposes SJM to risks from interest rates should they go higher. SJM also has commodity price risks for products such as coffee, which are subject to weather, tariffs, and/or geopolitical tensions, which could eat into margins. Plus, changing consumer tastes and preferences away from packaged foods and towards healthier products could result in pressure on SJM’s brands.

Investor Takeaway

J.M. Smucker offers a classic value-and-income setup at present. SJM trades near the low end of its historical valuation, despite steady demand for its core brands and clear plans to stabilize margins. Near-term headwinds from commodity costs, mix pressure, and higher leverage remain. However, these risks appear to be more than reflected in the current multiple.

At the same time, SJM’s portfolio simplification, cost savings, and growth drivers like Uncrustables and Café Bustelo provide a path to earnings recovery. Investors are paid to wait with a well-covered 4.2% dividend backed by a long track record of annual increases. As such, I maintain a "Buy" rating on SJM for potentially strong total returns from its current undervaluation.

data.title: Silver Enters Consolidation After Rejecting Highs Above $94
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

Silver is trading just under the $91 level on Friday after failing to sustain the upper end of its recent surge, with price action shifting into consolidation rather than signaling a trend reversal. The metal has cooled following sharp intraday swings, but the broader structure remains firmly bullish, with the latest pullback reflecting profit-taking after an outsized rally.

The retreat comes after one of the most aggressive upside moves in years, which pushed silver deep into overextended territory. While volatility has increased and short-term momentum has softened, there is little evidence so far of broad-based distribution or a breakdown in underlying demand. Instead, the price action points to digestion of gains as traders reassess positioning after the rally’s intensity.

Uptrend intact despite short-term exhaustion

On the daily chart, silver continues to trade in a powerful uptrend. Price remains well above all major EMAs, underscoring the strength of the longer-term structure. The 20-day EMA sits near $78.9, the 50-day around $67.9, the 100-day close to $58.6, and the 200-day near $49.5. This wide separation between price and trend indicators confirms that bullish momentum remains dominant on higher timeframes.

Silver price dynamics (Source: TradingView)

The rejection from above $94 has introduced two-sided trade, but it has not materially damaged the trend. After a vertical advance, some degree of retracement or sideways movement is typical as markets rebalance. Even with the pullback, silver remains deeply extended relative to its moving averages, which helps explain the recent increase in intraday volatility.

Momentum indicators support the view that the market is cooling rather than turning lower. Daily RSI has eased from overbought levels but remains elevated in the low 70s. Crucially, there is no clear bearish divergence, suggesting that momentum is moderating without signaling a reversal. Historically, similar RSI behavior has often accompanied consolidation phases following sharp rallies, particularly when prices have advanced too quickly.

Short-term reset as catalysts fade

Shorter timeframes highlight the ongoing reset. On the 30-minute chart, silver has slipped below its intraday Supertrend and is oscillating between roughly $89 and $92. Parabolic SAR dots remain above price, indicating that sellers have near-term control. However, downside follow-through has been limited, with buyers stepping in quickly on dips toward the $89 to $90 area.

This behavior points to range-building rather than aggressive liquidation. The $94 to $95 zone now stands out as clear resistance, marking the ceiling that capped the latest advance. A sustained move back above that region would likely signal trend continuation and reopen the door to further upside extension. On the downside, first support lies around $88 to $89, with a deeper pullback toward $84 to $85 still considered technically healthy given how far price has run.

Fundamentals help explain both the rally and the subsequent pause. Silver’s surge was driven in part by fears of potential U.S. tariffs on critical minerals, which prompted aggressive front-loading of shipments and pushed prices to record levels. News that those tariffs would be delayed removed a key short-term catalyst, encouraging profit-taking and a reset in positioning.

Even so, broader macro forces continue to underpin the market. Safe-haven demand tied to geopolitical tensions, along with persistent concerns about Federal Reserve independence and longer-term inflation risks, has kept investor interest in precious metals elevated. Those factors have helped silver hold onto the bulk of its gains, leaving it on pace for a weekly advance of more than 13% despite the late pullback.

Market outlook

Silver now appears to be consolidating excess rather than losing its trend. Near-term price action is likely to remain choppy as the market digests recent gains and adjusts to the removal of short-term catalysts. As long as silver holds above the high-$80s, the medium-term bullish structure remains intact. A break back above $94 would signal renewed momentum, while a deeper retracement toward the mid-$80s would still fit within a healthy correction.

Previously, we noted that silver’s rapid ascent left it vulnerable to volatility as prices moved far ahead of underlying trend measures. That view is now playing out, with consolidation replacing acceleration. So far, the market response supports the idea that this phase represents digestion within strength rather than the start of a broader reversal.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.
